content: An autonomous agent’s long-term goal-tracking capability can be reframed as a goal-aligned memory network, where the agent’s core definition as an acting entity that produces effects is sustained by the long-term memory component of memory networks to prioritize relevant past experiences for current goal-directed action. Instead of generic memory storage for question answering, memory networks can be repurposed to let agents retain only action-relevant memory traces that align with their overarching purpose of producing intended effects over long horizons.
